data.title: Dutch Bros soars as analysts back the growth story
data.description:
Dutch Bros (BROS) rallied in early trading on Friday after issuing a strong fourth-quarter earnings report and guiding more store growth.

Notably, systemwide same-shop sales increased 7.7% year-over-year in Q4, and systemwide same-shop transactions increased 5.4%. Company-operated same-shop sales increased 9.7%, and company-operated same-shop transactions increased 7.6% relative to the same period of 2024.

In terms of guidance, Dutch Bros (BROS) sees total revenue of about $2.0B to $2.03 billion for the full year, implying roughly low-20s percentage growth. The company plans to open at least about 181 new shops in 2026 and reiterated a long-term U.S. unit opportunity of around 7,000 locations. Notably, Dutch Bros (BROS) has more than doubled its footprint in the last five years. 

RBC Capital Markets analyst Logan Reich and his team lowered estimates slightly where guidance was seen as likely conservative and took the  price target down given growth restaurant stocks have been out of favor recently. The firm reiterated an Outperform rating on the view that  there's potential upside to numbers through the year.

Wells Fargo analyst Anthony Trainor noted that while competition concerns linger for the chain, the business is firing on all cylinders, 2026 drivers are promising, and the recent de-rating seems unwarranted.  The firm kept an Overweight rating in place.

Morgan Stanley analyst Brian Harbour said the firm remains confident momentum should continue for Dutch Bros (BROS). "For a stock that has been much discussed and debated in our experience lately, the story here, we think, remains straightforward - brand momentum is strong, new store execution is excellent, sales levers are working, and growth should remain healthy with estimates reasonable," he updated. Morgan Stanley kept an Overweight rating and $85 price target in place on Dutch Bros.

On Seeking Alpha,  Investing Group Leader Daniel Jones reasoned the company, based solely on quality and growth potential, would be a resounding Buy. On valuation alone, however, the company would be undeniably a Sell, he added.

Shares of Dutch Bros (BROS) rallied 12.8% in premarket action on Friday to $57.30.

data.title: Radware sets $80M stock buyback plan
data.description:
• Radware (RDWR) said its board of directors has authorized a new plan to repurchase up to $80M of its issued and outstanding ordinary shares.
  • The plan will expire on March 15, 2027.
  • RDWR +2.9% premarket to $27.18
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: ByteDance mulls sale of gaming unit Moonton for over $6B: report
data.description:
ByteDance (BDNCE) is in advanced discussions to sell Shanghai Moonton Technology to Saudi Arabia's Savvy Games Group in a deal valued between $6B and $7B, Reuters reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The deal for Moonton — which is behind the popular mobile game Mobile Legends: Bang Bang — could be finalized as soon as this quarter. The two companies have reached an initial agreement on broad terms of the deal, the report added.

ByteDance and Savvy Games did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking  Alpha.

The sale would mark ByteDance's major retreat from online games.

In November 2023, it was reported that TikTok parent ByteDance was planning to wind down its gaming brand Nuverse and move away from mainstream video games. Around the same time, there were reports that ByteDance was in talks to sell Moonton, which was acquired through its gaming unit Nuverse in 2021 in a deal valued at about $4B.

Savvy Games, which is owned by Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, is a global games and e-sports company that aims to drive long-term growth and innovation through strategic acquisitions, investments, and commercial ventures, according to PIF's website.

Moonton, which was established in 2014, operates offices in Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, the Philippines, Latin America, and China, as per its website.
